common_name: Million Hearts Plant (Heart Leaf Dischidia, Rosary Dischidia)

planting.note: Grow as a hanging basket, mounted epiphyte, or container plant in bright indirect to moderate shade; Million Hearts Plant cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. Mount on cork bark or tree fern slabs like an orchid for the most natural display, securing young stems with fishing line until roots establish. In a container, use a chunky mix of orchid bark and perlite with excellent drainage. Water thoroughly then allow to dry almost completely between waterings — overwatering causes rapid stem rot in this epiphyte. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade with exceptional drainage — Million Hearts Plant is a true epiphyte that rots quickly in heavy, moisture-retaining growing media. Dischidia ruscifolia is a small-leafed trailing epiphytic vine from the Philippines and Southeast Asia in the family Apocynaceae (the same family as Hoya and Stephanotis), producing thin, wiry, trailing stems clothed in pairs of tiny, heart-shaped, slightly fleshy, pale to medium-green leaves approximately half an inch across. The massed effect of hundreds of small green hearts cascading from a hanging basket gives the plant its popular common name — 'Million Hearts' — a name that perfectly captures the charming, delicate abundance of the foliage. As a true epiphyte from the humid forests of Southeast Asia, Dischidia grows naturally attached to rough tree bark in conditions of excellent air circulation, bright filtered light, and periodic drought — the small, slightly succulent leaves store water against dry periods between tropical rainstorms. Tiny, white, urn-shaped flowers appear sporadically along the stems. Multiple cultivars exist, including 'String of Nickels' (rounder, slightly larger leaves) and the slightly more compact 'Imbricata.' Non-toxic to pets and children.
sources: American Orchid Society — Dischidia and epiphytic vine cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dischidia ruscifolia for indoor use
Royal Horticultural Society — Dischidia cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Dischidia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Hoya pubicalyx (Silver Pink Hoya, Purple Rain Hoya, Wax Vine)

planting.note: Grow as a hanging basket or trellis container in bright indirect to moderate shade; Hoya pubicalyx cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 50°F. It grows faster than many hoyas and blooms more readily — often producing its first flower clusters within 1–2 years of a rooted cutting. Allow to become root-bound before repotting — Hoyas bloom more reliably when slightly pot-bound. Never remove old flower stalks (peduncles) as new flower clusters emerge from the same peduncle each season. Propagate readily from stem cuttings in moist mix.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade in a fast-draining, well-aerated potting mix; Hoya pubicalyx is one of the most vigorous and free-flowering of the commonly available Hoyas, producing its distinctive clusters of star-shaped flowers more readily than many other species. Hoya pubicalyx is a twining, semi-succulent epiphytic vine from the Philippines in the family Apocynaceae, producing long, trailing stems clothed in narrow, dark-green, lance-shaped leaves that are often lightly flecked or mottled with silver-gray spots — giving the species the common name 'Silver Pink Hoya.' The flowers are the defining ornamental feature: each umbellate cluster contains 20–40 individual star-shaped flowers in shades from deep burgundy-red to pink-purple (varying with cultivar and light intensity), each flower centered with a contrasting pink-white corona — the entire cluster has an almost candied, jewel-like appearance. The flowers exude a sweet, spicy, slightly chocolatey fragrance most intense in the evening. Multiple cultivars exist: 'Silver Pink' (dark burgundy-purple flowers), 'Red Buttons' (deep red flowers), 'Chimera' (variegated form). Like all Hoyas, the plant produces a milky latex sap when cut — avoid skin contact. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Hoya Society — Hoya pubicalyx care guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Hoya species cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hoya for indoor tropical culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Hoya pubicalyx (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Velvet Cardboard Anthurium (Clarinervium, White Veined Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or greenhouse specimen in bright indirect light; Anthurium clarinervium requires consistent warmth (65–85°F), high humidity (60–80%), and excellent drainage. Use a chunky, well-aerated epiphytic mix of orchid bark, perlite, and potting soil — never dense potting compost. Water thoroughly when the top layer of the mix dries; the plant is more drought-tolerant than humidity-tolerant, and chronic overwatering causes rapid root rot. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Anthurium clarinervium needs adequate light for healthy leaf production but will bleach and burn in direct sun. Anthurium clarinervium is a terrestrial to epiphytic anthurium endemic to the limestone cliff faces (karst) of Chiapas, Mexico, where it grows in the crevices and rock faces of cloud forest — an unusual natural habitat that explains its particular cultivation requirements: excellent drainage, high humidity, and bright filtered light in a calcareous substrate. The leaves are the defining ornamental feature: large, broad, heart-shaped, and thickly textured with a distinctive velvety or cardboard-like feel (giving the species the common name 'Cardboard Anthurium'), rich dark emerald-green to blue-green, covered with a network of precisely delineated, bright white to silvery-white raised veins in a complex branching pattern. Young leaves emerge in shades of rose to bronze before deepening to the mature dark green. The combination of bold leaf shape, velvety texture, and dramatic white venation has made Anthurium clarinervium one of the most sought-after velvet anthuriums among tropical plant collectors worldwide. The spathe flowers are small and inconsequential. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium clarinervium species notes
American Orchid Society — Epiphytic and lithophytic Anthurium cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Anthurium foliage plants for collectors
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)

common_name: Silver Monstera (Siltepecana Monstera, El Salvador Monstera)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio vine in zone 9; Monstera siltepecana cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Provide a moss pole, coco coir totem, or other rough climbing surface — the plant produces dramatically larger, more fenestrated leaves when allowed to climb upward, a phenomenon called 'juvenile vs. adult form' dimorphism that is extreme in this species. In the trailing or hanging basket form, the leaves remain in the small, silver-marked juvenile stage. Propagate from stem cuttings with at least one node and aerial root.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Silver Monstera is more tolerant of lower light than Monstera deliciosa but produces its best growth and most dramatic leaf size with consistent bright indirect light. Monstera siltepecana is a small-to-medium Monstera species native to southern Mexico (Chiapas, hence 'siltepecana' from the Siltepec region) south through Central America to El Salvador, producing one of the most striking examples of heterophylly (dramatic leaf-form difference between juvenile and adult stages) in the Araceae family. In the juvenile or trailing phase, the plant produces small (3–5 inch), elongated leaves with a distinctive silver or gray-blue metallic sheen overlaid on the dark green surface — silvery markings between the lateral veins that create an unmistakable iridescent effect, giving the species its common name 'Silver Monstera.' When provided a vertical climbing support and allowed to mature, the same plant produces dramatically larger adult leaves (12–24 inches) that develop fenestrations (holes) and lose the juvenile silver coloration entirely — a transformation so complete that juvenile and adult forms were historically treated as separate species. The juvenile silver form is what is sold commercially and is the reason for the plant's popularity. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Monstera siltepecana species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Monstera species for indoor tropical culture
American Horticulture Society — Heterophylly in Monstera: juvenile vs. adult forms
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Monstera (toxic to pets)

common_name: Black Gold Philodendron (Melanochrysum, Dark Gold Velvet Philodendron)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio climber in zone 9; Philodendron melanochrysum cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Provide a moss pole or coco coir totem to allow the plant to climb — vining philodendrons produce progressively larger and more dramatic leaves as they climb, while trailing specimens maintain smaller, less impressive leaves. Use a well-aerated mix of orchid bark, perlite, and potting soil. Maintain humidity above 60% for best appearance. Propagate from stem cuttings with a single node and aerial root. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Black Gold Philodendron needs consistent bright indirect light to produce its best, most dramatic foliage, but cannot tolerate direct sun which scorches the delicate velvet leaf surface. Philodendron melanochrysum is a climbing philodendron from the humid tropical forests of Colombia in the family Araceae, prized internationally as one of the finest velvet-leaved collector aroids. The leaves are large (reaching 24 inches or more on climbing specimens), elongated heart-shaped with a long, tapering tip, and velvety dark — a color so deep and saturated that it has been described as 'black-green' or 'midnight green' with a subtle sheen of bronze or gold visible in raking light, the quality that inspired the species name 'melanochrysum' (Greek: 'black gold'). The midrib and primary veins are precisely delineated in pale cream to yellowish-white, creating a striking contrast against the dark velvety surface. New leaves emerge in shades of bright copper-orange before slowly darkening to the mature near-black green. Philodendron melanochrysum has become one of the most desirable collector aroids of the current era, valued for both its exceptional leaf beauty and the dramatic transformation in leaf size as the plant matures on a climbing support. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron melanochrysum species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron climbing species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Velvet aroids: Philodendron collector varieties
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to pets)
